title: Histoplasmosis
authors: Chadi A. Hage, L. Joseph Wheat

items.title: Geographic Distribution and Risk Factors of Histoplasmosis
items.content: Histoplasmosis is most prevalent in North America, particularly along the Ohio and Mississippi river valleys. It is also endemic in parts of Mexico, Central and South America (notably Brazil), Africa, and Asia. The disease's distribution correlates with humid and acidic soil enriched by bird or bat droppings. High-risk activities include spelunking, excavation, and demolition work.
keyPoints: Most prevalent endemic mycosis in North America
Endemic areas: Ohio and Mississippi river valleys, parts of Mexico, Central and South America (Brazil), Africa, Asia
Soil enriched with bird or bat droppings promotes growth
High-risk activities: spelunking, excavation, cleaning chicken coops, demolition, remodeling old buildings, cutting dead trees

items.title: Changing Epidemiology of Histoplasmosis
items.content: The epidemiology of histoplasmosis is evolving due to global climate changes and increased international travel. This results in the disease appearing outside traditional endemic areas, often as imported cases in Europe from travelers or emigrants. The risk population is growing with more individuals on immunosuppressive therapies.
keyPoints: Epidemiology changing due to global climate change
Increased intercontinental and international travel spreads infection
Europe sees imported cases mostly from travelers/emigrants
Growing at-risk population: patients on immunosuppressive therapies for autoimmune disorders, cancers, organ transplants

items.title: Histoplasmosis Pathogenesis: Role of Macrophages and Cellular Immunity
items.content: In histoplasmosis, microconidia inhaled into the alveoli are engulfed by macrophages, transforming into yeasts. These yeasts evade immune detection and multiply within macrophages until cellular immunity develops around 2 weeks post-infection. T cells produce interferon-γ to aid macrophages in controlling the infection.
keyPoints: Microconidia transform into yeasts inside alveolar macrophages, dependent on calcium and iron.
Effective cellular immunity develops ~2 weeks after infection.
T cells produce interferon-γ to assist macrophages in killing H. capsulatum.
Interleukin 12 and TNF-α are crucial for cellular immunity against H. capsulatum.

items.title: Histoplasmosis Complications in Immunocompromised Hosts
items.content: In immunocompetent hosts, histoplasmosis typically results in granuloma formation that contains the organism. However, in patients with impaired cellular immunity, the infection can disseminate throughout the reticuloendothelial system, leading to progressive disseminated histoplasmosis (PDH) affecting multiple organs.
keyPoints: In immunocompetent hosts, granulomas form and often calcify.
Impaired cellular immunity leads to dissemination of infection.
Progressive disseminated histoplasmosis can affect lungs, bone marrow, spleen, liver, adrenal glands, and mucocutaneous membranes.
Unlike latent tuberculosis, inactive histoplasmosis does not reactivate.

items.title: Acute Histoplasmosis Symptoms and Radiographic Findings
items.content: In immunocompetent individuals with heavy exposure, acute histoplasmosis presents as a flulike illness. Symptoms include fever, chills, sweats, headache, myalgia, anorexia, dry cough, dyspnea, and chest pain. Chest radiographs typically show pneumonitis with hilar or mediastinal adenopathy. Pulmonary infiltrates can be focal or diffuse depending on exposure level.
keyPoints: Symptoms appear 1–4 weeks after exposure
Heavy exposure leads to flulike illness
Chest radiographs show pneumonitis and adenopathy
Pulmonary infiltrates: focal with light, diffuse with heavy exposure
Rheumatologic symptoms (arthralgia/arthritis) occur in 5–10% of patients

items.title: Disseminated Histoplasmosis (PDH) Risk Factors and Presentation
items.content: PDH is common in immunocompromised individuals, accounting for ~70% of cases. Risk factors include AIDS with CD4+ T-cell count <200/μL, use of immunosuppressive medications, and rare genetic immunodeficiencies. PDH can present acutely with rapid progression to respiratory failure or as a subacute/chronic illness with fever, weight loss, hepatosplenomegaly, thrombocytopenia, and other systemic manifestations.
keyPoints: PDH seen in ~70% of immunocompromised individuals
Risk factors: AIDS (CD4+ <200/μL), immunosuppressive medications
Acute PDH can cause respiratory failure, shock, coagulopathy, multiorgan failure
Subacute/chronic PDH presents with fever, weight loss, hepatosplenomegaly, thrombocytopenia

items.title: Chronic Cavitary Histoplasmosis Characteristics and Radiographic Findings
items.content: Chronic cavitary histoplasmosis occurs in smokers with structural lung disease. It is characterized by productive cough, dyspnea, low-grade fever, night sweats, and weight loss. Chest radiographs show upper-lobe infiltrates, cavitation, and pleural thickening, resembling tuberculosis.
keyPoints: Occurs in smokers with structural lung disease
Symptoms: productive cough, dyspnea, low-grade fever, night sweats, weight loss
Radiographic findings: upper-lobe infiltrates, cavitation, pleural thickening

items.title: Diagnostic Tools for Histoplasmosis: Antigen Detection vs. Culture
items.content: Antigen detection is preferred over culture and pathology due to its rapidity, non-invasiveness, and high sensitivity in diagnosing PDH and acute pulmonary histoplasmosis.
keyPoints: Culture remains the gold standard but takes up to 1 month for results; negative in less severe cases.
Antigen detection has a sensitivity >95% in PDH and >80% in severe acute pulmonary histoplasmosis with heavy exposure when both urine and serum are tested.
Lateral flow antigen detection allows point-of-care testing, beneficial in resource-limited areas.

items.title: Sensitivity of Histoplasma Antigen Detection by Body Fluids
items.content: Histoplasma antigen detection is highly sensitive and useful for diagnosis and monitoring disease progression in PDH.
keyPoints: Antigen levels correlate with severity and predict relapse; decrease with effective therapy.
Detection possible in CSF (meningitis) and BAL fluid, aiding rapid bedside diagnosis.

items.title: Serologic Tests for Histoplasmosis: Utility and Limitations
items.content: Serologic tests are useful in diagnosing histoplasmosis, particularly chronic pulmonary forms, but have limitations.
keyPoints: Immunodiffusion (ID) and complement fixation (CF) may take one month for antibody detection; EIA is more sensitive and detects IgM earlier than ID/CF.
Serologic tests are less sensitive in immunosuppressed patients and antibodies can persist post-infection, potentially leading to misdiagnosis.

items.title: When to Treat Histoplasmosis: Indications and Treatment Duration
items.content: Treatment is necessary for all patients with progressive disseminated histoplasmosis (PDH) or chronic pulmonary histoplasmosis, as well as most symptomatic patients with acute pulmonary histoplasmosis who have not improved by diagnosis time. In other cases of pulmonary histoplasmosis, treatment is generally unnecessary if the immune system is intact and exposure isn't heavy. Treatment should be considered if symptoms persist beyond one month.
keyPoints: Treatment indicated for PDH, chronic pulmonary histoplasmosis, and symptomatic acute pulmonary histoplasmosis without improvement by diagnosis time.
Consider treatment if symptoms do not improve within a month.
Duration of treatment: 6–12 weeks for acute pulmonary histoplasmosis; at least 1 year for PDH and chronic pulmonary histoplasmosis.

items.title: Preferred Treatment Regimens for Histoplasmosis: Medications and Monitoring
items.content: Lipid formulations of amphotericin B are preferred in severe cases, while itraconazole is used otherwise. Liposomal amphotericin B is favored over deoxycholate formulation due to better efficacy and tolerance, especially in AIDS and PDH patients. Itraconazole requires monitoring with target blood levels of 2–5 $ μg/mL for the parent drug and its hydroxy metabolites. Posaconazole and isavuconazole are alternatives if itraconazole is unsuitable.
keyPoints: Lipid formulations of amphotericin B preferred in severe cases; itraconazole used otherwise.
Target blood levels for itraconazole: 2–5 $ μg/mL$ for the parent drug and hydroxy metabolites.
Monitor drug interactions due to cytochrome P450 metabolism involvement by itraconazole.

items.question: Which geographic region is particularly noted for the endemicity of histoplasmosis in North America?
options: The Ohio and Mississippi river valleys
items.explanation: Histoplasmosis is particularly endemic in the Ohio and Mississippi river valleys of North America.

items.question: What type of soil conditions promote the growth and sporulation of Histoplasma?
options: Humid, acidic soils enriched with bird or bat droppings
items.explanation: The growth and sporulation of Histoplasma are promoted by humid, acidic soils enriched with bird or bat droppings.

items.question: Which activity is associated with a high infection attack rate for histoplasmosis?
items.explanation: Activities like spelunking and excavation are associated with high-level exposure and a high infection attack rate for histoplasmosis.

items.question: Which cells initially recognize and engulf microconidia during histoplasmosis infection?
items.explanation: Microconidia are rapidly recognized and engulfed by alveolar macrophages.

items.question: What is the role of T cells in controlling histoplasmosis infection?
options: They produce interleukin 12 to assist in killing the organism.
They produce interferon-γ to assist macrophages in killing the organism.
They form granulomas that contain the organisms.
They translocate yeasts to local draining lymph nodes.
items.explanation: T cells produce interferon-γ to assist the macrophages in killing the organism and controlling the progression of disease.

items.question: In immunocompetent hosts, what is the typical outcome for granulomas formed during histoplasmosis infection?
options: They lead to reactivation of inactive histoplasmosis.
items.explanation: In immunocompetent hosts, granulomas typically fibrose and calcify.

items.question: Which of the following symptoms are typically associated with acute histoplasmosis in immunocompetent individuals?
options: Fever, chills, sweats, headache, myalgia, anorexia, dry cough, dyspnea, and chest pain
Superior vena cava syndrome, obstruction of pulmonary vessels, and airway obstruction
items.explanation: Acute histoplasmosis in immunocompetent individuals presents with flulike symptoms such as fever, chills, sweats, headache, myalgia, anorexia, dry cough, dyspnea, and chest pain.

items.question: What is a common radiographic finding in asymptomatic lung nodules due to controlled histoplasmosis?
options: Upper-lobe infiltrates, cavitation, and pleural thickening
Diffuse interstitial or reticulonodular lung infiltrates
Signs of pneumonitis with prominent hilar or mediastinal adenopathy
Calcified mediastinal nodes or lung parenchymal nodules
items.explanation: Asymptomatic lung nodules due to controlled histoplasmosis are frequently found on chest CT scans.

items.question: Which of the following is a risk factor for progressive disseminated histoplasmosis (PDH) in immunocompromised individuals?
items.explanation: PDH is common in immunocompromised individuals, particularly those with AIDS and a CD4+ T-cell count <200/μL.

items.question: Which condition is characterized by fibrosis around hilar and mediastinal lymph nodes following acute histoplasmosis?
options: African histoplasmosis caused by var. duboisii
items.explanation: Fibrosing mediastinitis is characterized by progressive fibrosis around hilar and mediastinal lymph nodes.

items.question: Which diagnostic method is considered the gold standard for diagnosing histoplasmosis?
options: Antigen detection in urine and serum
items.explanation: Fungal culture remains the gold standard diagnostic test for histoplasmosis.

items.question: In which body fluid is Histoplasma antigen detection most useful for diagnosing PDH?
items.explanation: The sensitivity of Histoplasma antigen detection is >95% in patients with PDH when both urine and serum are tested.

items.question: What is the approximate sensitivity of antigen detection for severe acute pulmonary histoplasmosis if both urine and serum are tested?
items.explanation: The sensitivity of antigen detection is >80% in patients with severe acute pulmonary histoplasmosis resulting from heavy exposure.

items.question: Which serologic test method provides a more accurate way to monitor changes and antibody levels for diagnosing histoplasmosis?
options: Enzyme immunoassay (EIA) for IgG and IgM antibodies
items.explanation: EIA for IgG and IgM antibodies provides a more accurate method for monitoring changes and antibody levels.

items.question: Which antifungal agent is preferred as the initial treatment for severe histoplasmosis requiring hospitalization?
items.explanation: A lipid formulation of amphotericin B is used first for severe cases requiring hospitalization.

items.question: For which condition should a lipid formulation of amphotericin B be administered for 4–6 weeks before switching to itraconazole?
items.explanation: In patients with meningitis, a lipid formulation of amphotericin B should be given for 4–6 weeks before switching to itraconazole.

items.question: What is the target concentration range for itraconazole and its hydroxy metabolites in blood levels?
items.explanation: Blood levels of itraconazole should be monitored to ensure adequate drug exposure, with target concentrations of the parent drug and its hydroxy metabolites measuring 2–5 $ μ $g/mL.

items.statement: Histoplasmosis is increasingly reported outside of traditionally known endemic areas.
items.explanation: The epidemiology of histoplasmosis is changing, with increasing reports outside traditional endemic areas.

items.statement: Most cases of histoplasmosis in Europe are due to local transmission.
items.explanation: In Europe, most cases of histoplasmosis represent imported disease from travelers or emigrants.

items.statement: Histoplasmosis infection can reactivate in the same way as latent tuberculosis.
items.explanation: Unlike latent tuberculosis, inactive histoplasmosis does not reactivate.

items.statement: Structural lung disease can lead to chronic pulmonary histoplasmosis.
items.explanation: Structural lung disease impairs the clearance of pulmonary histoplasmosis, leading to chronic pulmonary disease.

items.statement: Chronic cavitary histoplasmosis typically affects individuals with structural lung disease such as bullous emphysema.
items.explanation: Chronic cavitary histoplasmosis is seen in smokers who have structural lung diseases like bullous emphysema.

items.statement: Histoplasmosis caused by var. duboisii primarily affects the lungs and mediastinum.
items.explanation: African histoplasmosis caused by var. duboisii is characterized by frequent skin and bone involvement, not primarily affecting the lungs and mediastinum.

items.statement: Histoplasma antigen can be detected in cerebrospinal fluid from patients with Histoplasma meningitis.
items.explanation: Histoplasma antigen can indeed be detected in cerebrospinal fluid from patients with Histoplasma meningitis.

items.statement: Serologic tests are most useful for diagnosing acute pulmonary histoplasmosis.
items.explanation: Serologic tests are especially useful for the diagnosis of chronic pulmonary histoplasmosis, not acute.

items.statement: Fluconazole is a preferred alternative to itraconazole for treating histoplasmosis in immunocompromised patients.
items.explanation: Histoplasma may develop resistance to fluconazole and voriconazole, and they are not the preferred alternatives to itraconazole.

items.statement: Antiretroviral treatment is recommended for improving outcomes in patients with PDH and AIDS.
items.explanation: Antiretroviral treatment improves the outcome of PDH in patients with AIDS and is recommended.

items.assertion: Acute histoplasmosis symptoms in immunocompetent individuals usually appear within a week after exposure.
items.reason: Symptoms of acute histoplasmosis develop 1–4 weeks after exposure.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason correctly states that symptoms typically appear 1-4 weeks after exposure.

items.assertion: Fibrosing mediastinitis can lead to fatal outcomes in some patients.
items.reason: Fibrosing mediastinitis is fatal in up to one-third of cases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why fibrosing mediastinitis can be fatal.

items.assertion: Antigen detection is more rapidly available than fungal culture.
items.reason: Culture results may take up to 1 month and are often negative in less severe cases, whereas antigen detection can be established more quickly.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why antigen detection is preferred for rapid diagnosis.

items.assertion: Histoplasma antigen testing has no cross-reactivity with other fungal infections.
items.reason: Cross-reactivity occurs with African histoplasmosis, blastomycosis, coccidioidomycosis, paracoccidioidomycosis, talaromycosis, and rarely aspergillosis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because cross-reactivity does occur with other fungal infections.

items.assertion: Liposomal amphotericin B should be used instead of deoxycholate formulation for most histoplasmosis cases.
items.reason: Liposomal amphotericin B is more effective and better tolerated than the deoxycholate formulation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why liposomal amphotericin B should be used instead of the deoxycholate formulation.
